Q1: I tried to keep a pretty structured study routine, dedicating about 26 hours a week spread across most days. Being a visual learner, I'd block out time to watch lecture videos and create diagrams or mind maps. With limited resources at home, I had to be strategic about when I could access materials, so I'd plan around when the internet was more reliable. I kept a simple planner where I'd write down what I needed to cover each week.
Q2: My pattern was mostly regular, but honestly it shifted a lot around deadlines. I'd have my baseline routine, but when assignments were due or exams were coming up, everything got more intense. Sometimes my extracurricular commitments would throw things off too, and I'd have to cram studying into whatever gaps I could find. I tried to stay consistent, but life doesn't always cooperate.
Q3: Usually I logged in to check if new video content was posted or to see announcements from instructors. Sometimes it was just to see what discussions were happening or if anyone had replied to my posts. When assignments were approaching, I'd log in more frequently to review requirements or check my grades. The peer support group I was part of also motivated me to stay connected.
Q4: Yeah, after the first few weeks, checking the LMS in the morning became almost automatic, like checking my phone. I think having 16 online courses made it necessary to stay on top of things. The peer support intervention helped too because I felt accountable to my study group. We'd coordinate through the discussion boards, so logging in became part of staying connected with them.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me since I learn best visually. I'd watch them multiple times, pausing to take notes or sketch out concepts. The discussion forums were also valuable because interacting with peers helped clarify things I was confused about. Having 32 peer interactions throughout the course really helped me understand material from different perspectives.
Q6: I'll be honest, I didn't always complete the readings or supplementary PDFs. With limited resources and time, I had to prioritize what would give me the most return. Some of the text-heavy documents just didn't stick with me the way videos did. I know my assignment completion was only around 46%, which isn't great, but I focused my energy on understanding concepts deeply for exams instead.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a direct connection to exam content or when it helped me genuinely understand something I was struggling with. Visual resources like diagrams, videos, or interactive elements always felt valuable. If something was just busywork or repetitive, I'd question whether it was the best use of my limited study time.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different. With required ones, there was pressure and a clear consequence for not doing them. Optional stuff I'd approach more selectively, choosing what seemed genuinely helpful rather than doing everything. Sometimes the optional activities were actually more interesting, but with my stress levels being high, I often had to skip them to focus on what was mandatory.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded videos and PDFs whenever I could because my internet isn't always reliable. I'd watch lectures offline, take handwritten notes, and create visual study guides on paper. I also used free YouTube videos and other external resources to supplement what the course offered. A lot of my real studying happened with printed materials spread across my desk.
Q10: Most of my deep learning happened offline with my notes and diagrams. I'd spend hours reviewing handwritten summaries or redrawing concept maps, none of which showed up in the LMS. When I was preparing for exams, I'd barely touch the platform because I was working from my own compiled materials. My 97.57 exam score came from that intensive offline preparation, not from clicking around online.
Q11: All the time. I'd watch a video once online, download it, and then rewatch it multiple times offline. Same with lecture slides, I'd print them out or save them to my phone and review them during commutes or when I didn't have internet. The LMS would only show that single initial access, but I might have studied that material for hours afterward.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, especially mid-semester when multiple courses had deadlines overlapping. My stress was really high during those times, and my motivation would dip. I'd feel overwhelmed and sometimes just couldn't bring myself to engage meaningfully. The peer support helped pull me through, but there were weeks where I was just surviving, not thriving.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted or stressed, I'd log in but not really absorb anything. Sometimes I had to choose between coursework and other responsibilities, and the LMS activities would suffer. Fatigue made it hard to focus on videos, and I'd find myself rewatching the same sections because nothing was sinking in. My moderate engagement level reflects those ups and downs.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd have videos playing but my mind was elsewhere, worrying about other things or just too tired to focus. I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them. Those sessions felt unproductive even though technically I was active on the platform. It's frustrating because the time was spent but the learning didn't happen.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I should have, which is why my completion rate was only 46%. Sometimes I'd avoid them because they felt overwhelming or I wasn't confident in my understanding. Other times, I prioritized exam preparation over assignments because exams were worth more. My low resilience meant that when things got tough, avoidance became my coping mechanism.
Q16: Deadlines basically dictated my LMS usage patterns. I'd have bursts of activity right before things were due, logging in frequently to submit work or double-check requirements. Without a deadline looming, my engagement would drop to maintenance level, just keeping up with new content. It wasn't the healthiest pattern, but it's how I managed with everything else going on.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. Positive feedback motivated me to keep going, but critical feedback sometimes made me anxious and I'd avoid that area for a while. When I got good exam scores, it boosted my confidence to engage more. But with my low mood overall, negative feedback could really set me back and make me question whether I was capable.
Q18: The course structure with clear modules and video content worked well for my visual learning style. Having discussion forums helped because peer interaction kept me accountable. But having 16 online courses simultaneously meant I couldn't give each one the attention it deserved. The structure was fine, but the volume was overwhelming at times.
Q19: Video-based content made engagement easier since that's how I learn best. Clear deadlines and organized modules helped me plan. What made it harder was when there was too much text-heavy content or when instructions were unclear. Also, some activities felt disconnected from what would actually be assessed, which made it hard to justify spending time on them.
Q20: I think that could happen when students are trying to understand material but struggling with the actual application. They might watch videos repeatedly, read discussions, and explore resources, but when it comes to assignments, they freeze up or run out of time. High activity could also mean they're looking for help or clarification rather than confidently completing work.
Q21: That actually describes me somewhat. My LMS activity was moderate, but I performed really well on exams because so much of my studying happened offline. If someone just looked at my login frequency or task completion, they might think I wasn't engaged. But I was deeply engaged with the material, just not in ways the system could track.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're active, what you click on, and submission times. But they miss the quality of engagement, whether you're actually learning or just going through motions. They can't capture offline studying, emotional state, or how much peer discussions outside the platform contributed. My exam success wouldn't be predicted by my moderate LMS engagement.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely trying to understand and connect with the material, not just completing tasks mechanically. It's about being curious, asking questions, and applying what you learn. For me, it also involves connecting with peers and feeling like part of a learning community. It's mental presence and effort, which isn't always visible in clicks and timestamps.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I relied on peer support outside the platform, the hours I spent with printed materials, or the stress I was managing throughout. It wouldn't capture my genuine interest in learning despite my low motivation scores, or how I strategically prioritized exam preparation over assignments. My distinction grade came from engagement that was mostly invisible to the system.
